title: Gelatine surface sizing for handmade paper
subtitle: Applying a warm gelatine size to finished sheets to give a writing surface
excerpt: A freshly dried handmade sheet is too absorbent for ink, a drop of water will spread into a blob within seconds. Surface sizing with a warm gelatine solution fills the fibres at the sheet surface and gives the paper the controlled absorbency that allows clean pen strokes. The same technique was used for all traditional European writing papers.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Papermaking: The History and Technique of an Ancient Craft (1943), Hunter discusses the history and practice of gelatine sizing in traditional European papermaking. The 1943 edition is in the public domain in the US. The gelatine sizing technique itself has been used continuously since the thirteenth century and is described in contemporary craft papermaking manuals as an unchanged procedure.

glossaryTerms.definition: The application of a gelatine or starch solution to the outer surface of a finished, dried paper sheet to reduce absorbency and give a controlled writing surface. Different from internal sizing (rosin + alum), which is added to the pulp before sheet formation.

A warm aqueous solution of food-grade gelatine (leaf or powder) used as a surface sizing agent for handmade paper. Sets to a firm, slightly glossy coating as it cools. The traditional surface size for European writing paper since the thirteenth century.
Paper that has been sized by dipping the finished sheet into a tray of warm size, as opposed to brush-applied surface sizing. Tub sizing gives a more even coat on both sides simultaneously.

content.text: turns a blotting-paper sheet into a writing surface. Without it, any water-based ink soaks in immediately and bleeds along the fibre paths, giving fuzzy edges and poor contrast. Gelatine fills the outer fibre layer without blocking it fully: the sized paper still feels like handmade sheet, but ink sits on the surface long enough for clean pen work.

items.name: Gelatine size, food-grade leaf gelatine or gelatine powder dissolved in warm water, 2 g per 100 ml
items.substitutions: 1% methyl cellulose solution for a reversible, non-animal alternative

content.text: Soak leaf gelatine in cold water for 5 minutes to soften it. Drain, then dissolve it in 100 ml of warm (not boiling) water per 2 g of gelatine. Do not boil, boiling degrades the protein and weakens the size.

into the tray. The size must be kept warm (around 40°C) during use, cool it and it will set to a gel before you finish the batch. Work near a warm room or set the tray over a shallow bowl of warm water.
Hold the dried sheet by two adjacent corners. Lower it slowly into the warm size from one end, angling slightly to avoid air bubbles. Submerge fully and hold for 10 seconds.
Lift the sheet by one corner and allow it to drain for a few seconds. The size should coat the paper evenly, no dry patches, no pools.
Lay the wet sheet flat on a press board and place a second press board on top. Press out excess size with gentle hand pressure, do not squeeze hard or the wet sheet will tear.

Hang or dry flat in a warm room until fully dry and the gelatine has set. The sheet will be slightly stiffer than before sizing. Write on it within 24 hours for best results; gelatine-sized paper can become tacky in high humidity if left too long before use.

items.symptom: The sheet is still too absorbent after sizing
items.cause: The size was too cool or too dilute when applied.
items.fix: Re-dip in fresh warm size. Check concentration, 2 g per 100 ml is the minimum effective ratio.

items.symptom: The sheet has a shiny, film-like coating
items.cause: Too much gelatine in the mix, or the sheet was not drained properly.
items.fix: Dilute the size further. After lifting from the tray, allow a longer drain time before pressing.
